one_liner: INTC -9.03% to $127 — giving back a record high set just yesterday at $142 — caught in the Meta cloud-driven chip selloff while fundamentally still executing its 18A process turnaround.

price_action.intraday_story: INTC opened at $135 (already down from $139 yesterday), made a brief attempt at $135.74, then sold off continuously to close near the low at $127.02. The 0.86x below-average volume on a near-9% decline is the key observation: this is more profit-taking and sector rotation than genuine fundamental selling. Institutional buyers are not panicking — they're stepping aside while momentum traders exit.

news_catalyst.headline: Meta cloud announcement drives sector-wide chip selloff; INTC gives back record gains one day after hitting $142.35 all-time high
news_catalyst.summary:
Intel hit a record high of $142.37 on June 30 — the culmination of a multi-month turnaround story — and gave back -9% the very next session. The proximate cause is Meta's plan to sell excess AI compute capacity, which the market interpreted as a signal that AI infrastructure spending by hyperscalers may be plateauing. While Intel's Xeon 6+ and Gaudi AI accelerators have exposure to data center demand, the actual business impact is indirect — Intel is a chip designer and foundry, not a cloud provider, and hyperscalers don't stop buying chips because one of them starts selling compute time.

The record high → immediate -9% structure also reflects the mechanics of a momentum-driven rally that hit its exhaustion point: Cantor Fitzgerald had just raised its price target to $150, the stock was extended, and the Meta headline gave large holders the excuse to take profits. This is a different situation from Micron, where the HBM demand narrative is more directly threatened by compute oversupply.

technical.summary:
Intel's Accumulation Phase B label from the engine is consistent with a stock that has been building a base since late 2025. The 90-day dataset shows INTC building from ~$118 (April 24 low with 2.55x volume — a possible Selling Climax) through a series of higher lows to yesterday's record $142.

Today's -9% decline on below-average volume reads as a Secondary Test (ST) of supply — the kind of volatility that characterizes Phase B accumulation. The April low at $118.50 is the critical Wyckoff support: as long as that level holds, the accumulation thesis remains intact. The intermediate support is the June 25 low at $125.41, which today's close barely held at $127.02.

One concern: the June 30 breakout to $142 was on below-average volume (0.90x) — a breakout that lacks volume conviction is suspect. Today's reversal confirms that $142 was not a true Sign of Strength but more likely a blow-off top within the range.

wyckoff_narrative:
The Wyckoff framework for INTC suggests we are in late Accumulation Phase B. The April 24 high-volume event (2.55x, 281M shares — the largest in the 90-day dataset) represents the most likely Selling Climax (SC) that initiated the accumulation range. The stock has since been building a series of higher lows, consistent with absorption of supply by patient institutional buyers.

Today's move from $142 record high to $127 has the character of a Last Point of Supply (LPSY) or an Upthrust After Distribution (UTAD) test — the market rallied to a new high only to be rejected sharply on the following session. If INTC can hold the $118.50 level over the next week and volume dries up on any further pullback, the Phase B accumulation remains intact and the next expected event is a Spring (bullish shakeout) before the eventual markup. The DCF is not feasible (Intel burning cash), so the fundamental support comes from the turnaround story and 18A process execution.

valuation.summary: DCF is not feasible — Intel is generating negative free cash flow during its foundry transition. Valuation is story-driven: Cantor raised PT to $150 citing AI infrastructure tailwinds and 18A process progress. The stock is a turnaround bet, not a value play.

key_strengths: Cantor Fitzgerald price target raised to $150
key_concerns: Negative free cash flow during foundry transition
Today's -9% suggests the record high was an extended top
fundamentals.summary: Intel's fundamental story is a turnaround narrative — the 18A process is progressing, Xeon 6+ is positioning for AI workloads, and institutional confidence (Cantor PT raise) was building. Today's selloff is more technical than fundamental, but the stock needs to prove it can hold the $125-127 zone.

risks: The June 25 intraday low of $125.41 is critical — a close below this level invites a test of $118.50 (April SC candidate low)
If Meta cloud plans materialize and hyperscaler AI capex genuinely slows, Intel's data center chip demand thesis weakens
A second consecutive down day tomorrow with volume pickup (>1.2x) would confirm the $142 was a distribution top, not an accumulation breakout
